title: Cloud Security and SaaS Governance Policy
content:
Cloud Security and SaaS Governance Policy v2.2 — Aligned with NIST SP 800-144, CSA Cloud Controls Matrix, and DoD Cloud Computing SRG.

1. CLOUD SERVICE APPROVAL
1.1 All cloud services (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS) must be approved by IT Security before procurement or use.
1.2 Cloud service requests must include a completed Cloud Security Assessment covering: data classification of information to be stored, geographic location of data storage, encryption capabilities, compliance certifications, incident response SLAs, and data portability/exit strategy.
1.3 Shadow IT (unauthorized cloud services) is prohibited. CASB (Cloud Access Security Broker) must be deployed to detect and block unapproved cloud usage.
1.4 SaaS applications must support SSO integration (SAML 2.0 or OIDC) and SCIM provisioning.

2. CLOUD SECURITY BASELINE
2.1 Identity and Access:
  a) Cloud admin accounts must use phishing-resistant MFA (per SEC-002)
  b) Root/owner accounts must not be used for daily operations; break-glass only
  c) Cloud IAM policies must follow least privilege and be reviewed quarterly
  d) Federated identity through enterprise IdP is mandatory; local cloud accounts are prohibited except for break-glass
2.2 Network:
  a) Virtual networks must be segmented by environment (production, staging, development) and workload sensitivity
  b) Default security groups must deny all inbound traffic
  c) Direct internet exposure of compute instances is prohibited; use load balancers, WAFs, and bastion hosts
  d) Private endpoints must be used for cloud service connectivity where available
2.3 Data:
  a) All data at rest must be encrypted using customer-managed keys (CMK)
  b) All data in transit must use TLS 1.2+
  c) Cloud storage buckets/blobs must be private by default; public access requires CISO approval
  d) Cross-region replication must maintain data residency requirements
2.4 Logging:
  a) Cloud audit logs (CloudTrail, Activity Log, Audit Log) must be enabled and exported to the enterprise SIEM
  b) Logs must be stored in immutable storage for minimum 1 year
  c) Flow logs must be enabled for all virtual networks

3. CLOUD CONFIGURATION MANAGEMENT
3.1 Cloud infrastructure must be managed as Infrastructure as Code (IaC) using approved tools (Terraform, CloudFormation, Pulumi).
3.2 Manual console changes to production cloud environments are prohibited except for emergency break-fix.
3.3 Cloud Security Posture Management (CSPM) must continuously monitor for misconfigurations.
3.4 CSPM findings rated Critical must be remediated within 24 hours; High within 7 days.
3.5 CIS Benchmarks for the cloud provider (AWS, Azure, GCP) must be the minimum security baseline.

4. CONTAINER AND SERVERLESS SECURITY
4.1 Container images must be scanned for vulnerabilities before deployment and at runtime.
4.2 Only images from approved container registries may be deployed to production.
4.3 Container runtime security monitoring must be deployed for anomaly detection.
4.4 Serverless functions must follow least-privilege IAM roles scoped to specific resources.
4.5 Serverless function timeouts and concurrency limits must be configured to prevent abuse.

5. MULTI-CLOUD AND HYBRID CONSIDERATIONS
5.1 Security controls must be consistent across cloud providers. A cloud-agnostic security framework must be maintained.
5.2 Network connectivity between cloud providers and on-premises must use encrypted tunnels (IPSec VPN or dedicated interconnects).
5.3 Centralized identity management must span all cloud environments.
5.4 A unified SIEM must aggregate logs from all cloud and on-premises environments.

6. VENDOR MANAGEMENT
6.1 Cloud providers must maintain SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001, or FedRAMP authorization.
6.2 Cloud vendor security assessments must be reviewed annually.
6.3 Cloud service SLAs must define: availability guarantees, incident notification timelines, data breach responsibilities, and data return/deletion upon contract termination.
6.4 Data Processing Agreements (DPAs) must be executed for any cloud service processing PII.

7. REFERENCES
- NIST SP 800-144 Guidelines on Security and Privacy in Public Cloud Computing
- NIST SP 800-210 General Access Control Guidance for Cloud Systems
- CSA Cloud Controls Matrix v4.0
- DoD Cloud Computing Security Requirements Guide (SRG)
- CIS Benchmarks for AWS, Azure, GCP
- ISO 27017:2015 Cloud Security
